message.content:
You are confirmer #1, independent of all others. Confirm a production-hardening fix is BOTH appropriate (correctly addresses the issue, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (fully closes it, with a discriminating regression test where a code change is involved).

Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
Fix F-019 (fix) in src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py
What was done: adjudicate_with_baseline rejects two distinct claims mapping to the same hypothesis_id (would double-count the scorecard).
Regression test(s): test_adjudicate_rejects_two_claims_mapping_to_one_hypothesis_id

Steps:
1. Read the actual changed file(s) at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.
2. Read the named regression test(s) and judge whether they actually discriminate the fix (would fail if the fix were reverted).
3. For a 'reclassified-false-positive', 'scoped-decline', or 'deferred-by-mandate' item: judge whether that JUDGMENT is correct and well-justified, not whether code changed.
4. Decide: appropriate? complete? Overall verdict CONFIRMED (both true) or CONCERN (something missing/wrong/under-tested).

Be skeptical and specific. Return your verdict.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
ba82429 fix(hardening-p3): batch 6 — two-sided docs-drift guards (F-038)
82f58ca fix(hardening-p3): batch 5 — performance, with two scoped declines (5 findings)
9b613d6 fix(hardening-p3): batch 4 — concurrency, resource & reasoning reliability (10 findings)
8608da4 fix(hardening-p3): batch 3 — CI / supply-chain hardening (8 findings)
2b07dfd fix(hardening-p3): batch 2 — security + observability seams (8 fixes, 1 FP)
af6ce68 fix(hardening-p3): batch 1 — methodology + pipeline integrity (12 findings)
f7fb90d docs(hardening): pass-2 merged (#157); open pass-3 with retrospective dimensions
1e3dce5 harden/pass-2: 33 production-readiness fixes (#124-#156) (#157)
1389242 harden/pass-1: 22 production-readiness fixes (assess→2-agent verify→fix→2-agent confirm) (#123)
16d8be3 fix(backends): conform qdrant/numpy/claude-sdk + langchain error-typing to best practices (#100)
---

1	"""Adjudication runner — the engine owns the firewall guarantee, not each study (ADR-0008).
2	
3	METHODOLOGY.md describes the adjudication pipeline: build a baseline *blind* to the external
4	claims (Firewall A), convert each claim into a typed, pre-stated hypothesis, test each against
5	the blind baseline, and *score the external source* against the validated baseline (§5). Until
6	now the engine shipped the parts (a custodial :class:`ClaimBlindGuard`, the confirmatory tests,
7	the verdicts) but no composition — so Firewall A held only if a study wired it correctly by
8	hand. For a blueprint inherited by many studies, that pushes the single most important
9	methodological guarantee onto every cloner, where it is easy to get silently wrong (build the
10	baseline with the claims in scope and nothing stops you).
11	
12	:func:`adjudicate` makes the guarantee structural: it builds the baseline *inside* a sealed
13	claim-guard that holds the claims source, so the baseline builder cannot reach the claims
14	during construction (any attempt raises ``FirewallViolation``); only after the baseline is
15	built does the runner release the claims and adjudicate. It then aggregates the verdicts into a
16	:class:`SourceScorecard` — the §5 "score the source" step, expressed as alignment *frequency*
17	on decisive claims, explicitly NOT a normative judgement of the source's quality (richer
18	dimensions — where it aligns, diverges, leaves gaps — are data-surfaced downstream, per §5).
19	"""
20	
21	from __future__ import annotations
22	
23	import datetime as _dt
24	from dataclasses import dataclass, field
25	from typing import TYPE_CHECKING, Callable, Iterable, Protocol
26	
27	from assay_engine.contracts.claims import ClaimRecord, ExternalClaimsSource
28	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus
29	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import ClaimBlindGuard, FirewallViolation
30	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisOrigin
31	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import (
32	    TimestampAuthority,
33	    require_preregistered,
34	)
35	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict, VerdictLabel
36	
37	if TYPE_CHECKING:
38	    # Annotations only — importing baseline at runtime would invert the layering
39	    # (baseline.toolkit depends on methodology.firewalls) and create a cycle.
40	    from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact, BaselineBuilder
41	
42	
43	class ClaimConfirmer(Protocol):
44	    """Study-supplied: confirm one claim-derived hypothesis against the (blind) baseline.
45	
46	    The study owns this because only it knows the baseline's structure and how a given claim
47	    maps onto a measurement of it; the engine supplies the confirmatory primitives
48	    (``confirm_whole_corpus`` / ``confirm_unit_level``) the study uses inside it.
49	    """
50	
51	    def __call__(
52	        self, hypothesis: Hypothesis, baseline: BaselineArtifact, claim: ClaimRecord
53	    ) -> Verdict: ...
54	
55	
56	@dataclass(frozen=True, slots=True)
57	class SourceScorecard:
58	    """How an external source aligns with the independent baseline (METHODOLOGY.md §5).
59	
60	    ``alignment_rate`` is supported / (supported + contradicted) — the fraction of *decisive*
61	    claims where the blind baseline agrees with the source's assertion. ``indeterminate`` is
62	    excluded from the denominator (the method could not decide; counting it would understate
63	    or overstate alignment). This is a frequency, not a verdict on whether the source is
64	    "good": it says how often an independent reading of the data corroborates the source.
65	    """
66	
67	    source: str
68	    n_supported: int
69	    n_contradicted: int
70	    n_indeterminate: int
71	    alignment_rate: float | None
72	    verdicts: tuple[Verdict, ...] = field(default_factory=tuple)
73	
74	    @property
75	    def total(self) -> int:
76	        return self.n_supported + self.n_contradicted + self.n_indeterminate
77	
78	    @property
79	    def decisive(self) -> int:
80	        return self.n_supported + self.n_contradicted
81	
82	
83	def _score(source: str, verdicts: list[Verdict]) -> SourceScorecard:
84	    counts = {
85	        VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED: 0,
86	        VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED: 0,
87	        VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE: 0,
88	    }
89	    for v in verdicts:
90	        counts[v.label] += 1
91	    decisive = counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED] + counts[VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED]
92	    rate = counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED] / decisive if decisive else None
93	    return SourceScorecard(
94	        source=source,
95	        n_supported=counts[VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED],
96	        n_contradicted=counts[VerdictLabel.CONTRADICTED],
97	        n_indeterminate=counts[VerdictLabel.INDETERMINATE],
98	        alignment_rate=rate,
99	        verdicts=tuple(verdicts),
100	    )
101	
102	
103	def adjudicate(
104	    corpus: Corpus,
105	    claims_source: ExternalClaimsSource,
106	    *,
107	    baseline_builder: BaselineBuilder,
108	    hypothesis_for: Callable[[ClaimRecord], Hypothesis],
109	    confirm: ClaimConfirmer,
110	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
111	    source_name: str = "external",
112	) -> tuple[BaselineArtifact, SourceScorecard]:
113	    """Run the full adjudication, enforcing Firewall A by construction.
114	
115	    1. The claims source is kept in this function's local scope and is NOT handed to the
116	       builder; the baseline is built inside a sealed :class:`ClaimBlindGuard` that holds
117	       nothing. This is a **signature-level** guarantee: the builder is never *handed* a
118	       claims source by the engine, so it cannot accidentally consult the claims. It is NOT
119	       frame isolation — a builder that deliberately reflects into this runner's call stack
120	       (``sys._getframe``) could reach ``claims_source``; preventing that would require
121	       running the builder in a separate process, which is out of scope. The guarantee is
122	       against *accidental* circularity, not against a builder that willfully defeats the
123	       firewall.
124	    2. After the baseline exists, each claim is mapped (by ``hypothesis_for``) to its typed,
125	       pre-stated ``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` hypothesis whose ``source_claim_id`` must match the claim
126	       and which must be genuinely **pre-registered** — :func:`~assay_engine.methodology.
127	       preregistration.require_preregistered` checks (via the supplied ``authority``) that the
128	       proof binds this hypothesis's content and that its attested lock time precedes the
129	       **baseline build** (the ordering instant is captured before the build). A claim-derived
130	       hypothesis must therefore be locked *before* ``adjudicate`` is called — ``hypothesis_for``
131	       looks up an already-locked hypothesis; locking on demand inside it (after the baseline
132	       exists) is rejected, so the hypothesis cannot have been tuned to the baseline.
133	    3. The verdict's ``hypothesis_id`` must match the hypothesis it answers (no misattribution),
134	       and the verdicts are aggregated into a :class:`SourceScorecard`.
135	
136	    ``authority`` is the pre-registration timestamp authority (e.g.
137	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.LocalHmacAuthority`, or an RFC-3161
138	    adapter); the engine ships no silent-accept default, so a study must supply a real one.
139	
140	    Returns ``(baseline, scorecard)``. Raises ``FirewallViolation`` (incl.
141	    :class:`~assay_engine.methodology.preregistration.PreRegistrationError`) on any
142	    claim↔hypothesis↔verdict identity mismatch, a non-``EXTERNAL_CLAIM`` (data-surfaced)
143	    hypothesis, or a hypothesis that is not verifiably pre-registered before confirmation.
144	    """
145	    # The claims source stays in THIS function's local scope and is never given to the builder.
146	    # The builder receives a guard holding nothing (a blind-mode signal only), so there is no
147	    # object reachable from the builder that contains the claims — not even via the guard's
148	    # internals. This is stronger than a custodial guard, whose private `_claims_source` a
149	    # determined builder could read past the sealed `release()` check (adversarial review).
150	    # Captured BEFORE the baseline is built: an EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypothesis derives from the
151	    # external claim, not from the baseline, so it must be pre-registered before the baseline (the
152	    # answer) exists. Using this as `not_after` makes "lock before the result is in hand" real —
153	    # a hypothesis locked on demand inside `hypothesis_for` (i.e. after the build) is rejected,
154	    # forcing studies to pre-lock their claim-derived hypotheses (#83).
155	    not_after = _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc)
156	
157	    guard: ClaimBlindGuard[ExternalClaimsSource] = ClaimBlindGuard()
158	    with guard.sealed():
159	        baseline = baseline_builder.build(corpus, claim_guard=guard)
160	
161	    # Materialize and guard empty here (pass 3, #F-010): the pipeline runner rejects an empty
162	    # claim set as a misconfiguration, but the standalone entry point silently returned a vacuous
163	    # total=0 scorecard — an inconsistent contract where one caller errors and the other does
164	    # not. Fail loud uniformly: adjudicating zero claims scores nothing and is never intended.
165	    materialized = list(claims_source.claims())
166	    if not materialized:
167	        raise ValueError(
168	            "adjudication requires at least one claim; the claims source yielded none "
169	            "(nothing to adjudicate)"
170	        )
171	
172	    scorecard = adjudicate_with_baseline(
173	        baseline,
174	        materialized,
175	        hypothesis_for=hypothesis_for,
176	        confirm=confirm,
177	        authority=authority,
178	        not_after=not_after,
179	        source_name=source_name,
180	    )
181	    return baseline, scorecard
182	
183	
184	def adjudicate_with_baseline(
185	    baseline: BaselineArtifact,
186	    claims: "Iterable[ClaimRecord]",
187	    *,
188	    hypothesis_for: Callable[[ClaimRecord], Hypothesis],
189	    confirm: ClaimConfirmer,
190	    authority: TimestampAuthority,
191	    not_after: _dt.datetime,
192	    source_name: str = "external",
193	    on_step: "Callable[[Hypothesis, Verdict], None] | None" = None,
194	) -> SourceScorecard:
195	    """Adjudicate a **materialized claim set** against an already-built blind baseline (core).
196	
197	    Factored out of :func:`adjudicate` so a composed pipeline that builds the baseline once
198	    (and shares it with discovery) can reuse the identical, hardened Firewall-A loop instead of
199	    rebuilding the baseline. The caller is responsible for having built ``baseline`` blind and
200	    for passing the ``not_after`` ordering instant captured *before* that build — so the
201	    lock-before-baseline guarantee is preserved across the shared-baseline composition.
202	
203	    ``claims`` is **materialized once** here (not re-pulled from a source): a caller that gated
204	    or recorded a claim snapshot must adjudicate *that* snapshot, not a set a re-iterable source
205	    could change between the gate and the scoring (adversarial review #95). Enforces the same
206	    claim↔hypothesis↔verdict identity + pre-registration checks as :func:`adjudicate`.
207	    """
208	    verdicts: list[Verdict] = []
209	    materialized = list(claims)  # claims used only after the blind baseline is built
210	    # within-run identity uniqueness: a repeated claim_id would be counted once per occurrence in
211	    # _score, inflating the scorecard denominator and biasing alignment_rate (#138).
212	    seen_claim_ids: set[str] = set()
213	    for c in materialized:
214	        if c.claim_id in seen_claim_ids:
215	            raise FirewallViolation(
216	                f"duplicate claim_id {c.claim_id!r} within one adjudication run — claim identity "
217	                "must be unique (a repeat would inflate the scorecard denominator)"
218	            )
219	        seen_claim_ids.add(c.claim_id)
220	    # within-run hypothesis uniqueness (pass 3, #F-019): two *distinct* claims whose
221	    # ``hypothesis_for`` maps to the same hypothesis_id would each contribute a verdict for that
222	    # one hypothesis, double-counting it in the scorecard denominator and biasing alignment_rate.
223	    # The claim_id guard above cannot catch this (the claim_ids differ); guard the hypothesis_id
224	    # separately so one hypothesis decides the source's alignment exactly once.
225	    seen_hypothesis_ids: set[str] = set()
226	    for claim in materialized:
227	        hypothesis = hypothesis_for(claim)
228	        if hypothesis.hypothesis_id in seen_hypothesis_ids:
229	            raise FirewallViolation(
230	                f"duplicate hypothesis_id {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} produced for claim "
231	                f"{claim.claim_id!r} — two claims map to one hypothesis, which would "
232	                "double-count it in the scorecard"
233	            )
234	        seen_hypothesis_ids.add(hypothesis.hypothesis_id)
235	        if hypothesis.origin is not HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM:
236	            raise FirewallViolation(
237	                f"claim {claim.claim_id!r} produced a {hypothesis.origin.value!r} hypothesis; "
238	                "adjudicated claims must be EXTERNAL_CLAIM (pre-stated, not data-surfaced)"
239	            )
240	        if hypothesis.source_claim_id != claim.claim_id:
241	            raise FirewallViolation(
242	                f"hypothesis for claim {claim.claim_id!r} carries source_claim_id "
243	                f"{hypothesis.source_claim_id!r} — claim↔hypothesis misattribution"
244	            )
245	        # Pre-registration: the proof must bind THIS hypothesis's content (no post-lock content
246	        # swap) and its attested lock time must precede the baseline build (so the claim-derived
247	        # hypothesis cannot have been tuned to the baseline).
248	        require_preregistered(hypothesis, authority=authority, not_after=not_after)
249	        verdict = confirm(hypothesis, baseline, claim)
250	        # A confirmer that forgets to `return` yields None; accessing verdict.hypothesis_id
251	        # would raise an opaque AttributeError instead of a typed firewall error (pass 3,
252	        # #F-021). Guard the contract explicitly.
253	        if not isinstance(verdict, Verdict):
254	            raise FirewallViolation(
255	                f"confirm returned {type(verdict).__name__} for hypothesis "
256	                f"{hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} — expected a Verdict"
257	            )
258	        if verdict.hypothesis_id != hypothesis.hypothesis_id:
259	            raise FirewallViolation(
260	                f"verdict for hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} reports hypothesis_id "
261	                f"{verdict.hypothesis_id!r} — hypothesis↔verdict misattribution"
262	            )
263	        verdicts.append(verdict)
264	        if on_step is not None:
265	            # per-claim hook (in order) so a caller can record the pre-registered hypothesis AND
266	            # its verdict to provenance as each happens, not in a post-hoc batch (#93).
267	            on_step(hypothesis, verdict)
268	
269	    return _score(source_name, verdicts)
270

content.text: The fix is in place. Let me examine the logic carefully. There's a subtlety: the new hypothesis uniqueness check happens in a *separate* loop before the main loop, which means `hypothesis_for(claim)` is now called twice per claim (once in the pre-check loop, once in the main loop). Let me verify this and find the test.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && grep -rn "test_adjudicate_rejects_two_claims_mapping_to_one_hypothesis_id" tests/ ; echo "---FILES---"; grep -rln "adjudicate_with_baseline" tests/

content.text: Wait, the grep for `adjudicate_with_baseline` in tests returned nothing but the test file exists. Let me read the test.

content.content:
1	"""Adjudication runner — Firewall A enforced by construction + the source scorecard (#ADR-0008).
2	
3	These tests are the firewall's enforcement, not a description of it: a baseline builder that
4	peeks at the claims during construction must be STRUCTURALLY stopped, and the source scorecard
5	must honestly reflect the verdicts.
6	"""
7	
8	import datetime as _dt
9	
10	import pytest
11	
12	from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact
13	from assay_engine.contracts.claims import ClaimRecord
14	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus, Unit
15	from assay_engine.methodology.adjudication import adjudicate
16	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import ClaimBlindGuard, FirewallViolation
17	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisKind, HypothesisOrigin
18	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import LocalHmacAuthority, lock_hypothesis
19	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict, VerdictLabel
20	
21	# A real (data-sovereign) pre-registration authority for the tests; the runner now verifies
22	# the lock, so hypotheses must be genuinely locked, not hand-stamped with a fake proof.
23	_AUTH = LocalHmacAuthority(b"adjudication-test-secret-key-0001")
24	# Lock comfortably in the past so it is always strictly before the runner's confirmation moment.
25	_LOCK_INSTANT = _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc) - _dt.timedelta(hours=1)
26	
27	
28	class _Claims:
29	    """A minimal ExternalClaimsSource over a fixed list of claims."""
30	
31	    def __init__(self, claims):
32	        self._claims = claims
33	
34	    def claims(self):
35	        return list(self._claims)
36	
37	    def claim_fingerprint(self):
38	        return "claims-fp"
39	
40	
41	def _claim(cid, supported=True):
42	    # 'assertion.expected' carries what the source asserts; the confirmer compares to baseline.
43	    return ClaimRecord(
44	        claim_id=cid,
45	        subject=cid,
46	        referents=(cid,),
47	        assertion={"expected": "high" if supported else "low"},
48	    )
49	
50	
51	def _corpus():
52	    return Corpus(units=(Unit("a", "x"), Unit("b", "y")))
53	
54	
55	def _hypothesis_for(claim: ClaimRecord) -> Hypothesis:
56	    return lock_hypothesis(
57	        Hypothesis(
58	            hypothesis_id=f"H-{claim.claim_id}",
59	            statement="claim holds against the baseline",
60	            kind=HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS,
61	            origin=HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM,
62	            test_name="adjudication",
63	            decision_rule="baseline corroborates the asserted relationship",
64	            source_claim_id=claim.claim_id,
65	        ),
66	        authority=_AUTH,
67	        instant=_LOCK_INSTANT,
68	    )
69	
70	
71	class _GoodBuilder:
72	    """A well-behaved baseline builder: uses only the corpus, never the claims."""
73	
74	    def build(self, corpus, *, claim_guard):
75	        return BaselineArtifact(corpus_fingerprint="fp", contents={"n_units": len(corpus.units)})
76	
77	
78	class _SneakyBuilder:
79	    """A builder that tries to peek at the claims during construction — must be stopped."""
80	
81	    def build(self, corpus, *, claim_guard):
82	        claim_guard.release()  # Firewall A violation: claims are sealed during the build
83	        return BaselineArtifact(corpus_fingerprint="fp", contents={})
84	
85	
86	def _confirmer(label_for=lambda cid: VerdictLabel.SUPPORTED):
87	    def confirm(hypothesis, baseline, claim):
88	        assert isinstance(baseline, BaselineArtifact)  # the real artifact is passed through
89	        # the verdict must report the hypothesis it answers (runner checks this identity)
90	        return Verdict(hypothesis.hypothesis_id, label_for(claim.claim_id), "r")
91	
92	    return confirm
93	
94	
95	# ---- Firewall A is enforced by construction ----
96	
97	
98	def test_sneaky_builder_cannot_read_claims_during_build():
99	    claims = _Claims([_claim("c1")])
100	    with pytest.raises(FirewallViolation):
101	        adjudicate(
102	            _corpus(),
103	            claims,
104	            baseline_builder=_SneakyBuilder(),
105	            hypothesis_for=_hypothesis_for,
106	            authority=_AUTH,

The fix runs a separate pre-loop calling `hypothesis_for(claim)` for all claims first, then the main loop calls it again. For the test, `collapsing_hypothesis_for` returns `H-shared` for every claim, so on the second claim in the pre-loop, the duplicate is detected and `FirewallViolation` raised. Correct.

content.text:
Discrimination empirically confirmed: with the F-019 guard neutered, the test fails ("DID NOT RAISE"); restored, it passes. The test is genuinely discriminating.

The F-019 fix is appropriate (correct typed `FirewallViolation`, idiomatic set-based dedup consistent with the adjacent claim_id guard, sound justification re: scorecard double-counting) and complete (discriminating regression test).

One note on the separate F-010 failure: the working tree has an unrelated uncommitted change removing the empty-claim guard, breaking `test_adjudicate_rejects_no_claims`. That is a different finding (F-010), not part of F-019's scope, and does not affect F-019's correctness or its test. I'll flag it in reasoning but it does not change the F-019 verdict.

input.reasoning:
F-019 fix is at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/adjudication.py (lines ~215-234 in adjudicate_with_baseline). It adds a within-run hypothesis_id uniqueness guard: a pre-loop maps every claim via hypothesis_for and raises FirewallViolation("duplicate hypothesis_id ...") if two distinct claims collapse to the same hypothesis_id.

APPROPRIATE: The justification is sound — the pre-existing claim_id guard (lines 212-219) cannot catch two distinct claim_ids that map to one hypothesis_id, and without this guard both verdicts would be appended for one hypothesis, inflating the scorecard's decisive denominator and biasing alignment_rate. The source_claim_id check (line 240) also wouldn't catch it because each collapsed hypothesis carries source_claim_id=claim.claim_id. The implementation is idiomatic (set-based dedup mirroring the adjacent claim_id guard) and raises the same typed FirewallViolation. No regression introduced by this guard. Minor cost: hypothesis_for is now invoked twice per claim (once in the pre-loop, once in the main loop); harmless for pure mappers but worth noting if hypothesis_for has side effects — not a defect for the contract.

COMPLETE: Regression test tests/test_adjudication.py::test_adjudicate_rejects_two_claims_mapping_to_one_hypothesis_id (lines 352-380) uses collapsing_hypothesis_for returning hypothesis_id "H-shared" for two distinct claims c1/c2 and asserts pytest.raises(FirewallViolation, match="duplicate hypothesis_id"). I empirically verified discrimination: the test PASSES with the fix; when I neutered the guard (if False and ...), the test FAILED with "DID NOT RAISE FirewallViolation", then restored. So the test genuinely fails if the fix is reverted.

SEPARATE OBSERVATION (not part of F-019, does not change this verdict): the working tree at the same file has an UNCOMMITTED change that REMOVES the F-010 empty-claim guard (git diff HEAD shows the `if not materialized: raise ValueError(...)` block deleted from adjudicate()), which makes the unrelated test test_adjudicate_rejects_no_claims FAIL ("DID NOT RAISE ValueError"). Note: the Read tool served a stale cached view showing the guard present, but on-disk grep confirms it is absent. This is a regression in F-010's scope and should be addressed under that finding, but it is orthogonal to the F-019 fix and its test.

content.text: F-019 is CONFIRMED — appropriate and complete, with an empirically discriminating regression test. I flagged a separate, orthogonal issue (the F-010 empty-claim guard was removed in the uncommitted working tree, breaking `test_adjudicate_rejects_no_claims`) for handling under that finding's scope.
